title: Shin as a conclusion to EoE
display_title: Shin as a conclusion to EoE
wikitext:
[]: Shin as a conclusion to EoE             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Tue Mar 23, 2021 3:12 am I know everything is still up to debate, whether meta ending or real ending, whatever, doesn't matter to me, but do you guys think it's a good ending for it? Would you accept it as the conclusion? How would you feel if it was? Would you feel let down ? Indifferent?

[]: Re: Shin as a conclusion to EoE             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Tue Mar 23, 2021 3:27 am EoE is definitive on its own. I can't think of a single thing from reading Shin's script where anything truly needed was added. I know there's lots of talk of "but Shinji never had a sitdown-type conversation with Asuka, Rei, etc. before," but some things are better left unsaid or implied. EoTV and EoE's instrumentalities where Shinji was engaging with other characters' repressed psychological pressure points was far more meaningful and revelatory than hashing it out in some kind of Oprah-style "all my problems were because of X and now I'm a changed person" wank. Real people still have to live with lingering pangs of prior destructive behaviors even if they move on and mature.There's no cheerful "just getting over it." Ironically, Shin's optimistic ending seems more cynical to me than EoE. "Things will be hard, get better, and get hard again, but to embrace that and move on in spite of it is the only meaningful option" is a way better parting shot than "just be more extroverted, get visibly excited, hold hands with a girl with big breasts and run into Anno's home town."

[]: Re: Shin as a conclusion to EoE             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Mar 23, 2021 3:37 am i dont think thats what it was saying though. imho it was a good choice to sort of imply some continuity but NOT make it explicit or definite, because ppl are going to project in stuff from the old series anyways, but at the same time it wouldn't be good to muck up a perfectly fine self-contained ending. pitting it against each other (& focussing on EoE in particular) seems pointless, I doubt one-upmanship or improvement was the intention behind it. I mean EoE still exists, TuaT is not going to travel back in time & lazer it into nonexistence, and there'd be no point in repeating it or doig what it's already done just fine. The point here seems to reach a different kind of catharsis than the one we already got. I don't see how "my friends helped me & now I'm in a place where I want to take action & help others in turn" as an autobiographical feeling, is any less authentic than "everything sucks but I think regardless still want to go on"; They belong to different stages at life, probably.

[]: Re: Shin as a conclusion to EoE             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Tue Mar 23, 2021 4:20 am Kendrix wrote: I don't see how "my friends helped me & now I'm in a place where I want to take action & help others in turn" as an autobiographical feeling, is any less authentic than "everything sucks but I think regardless still want to go on"; They belong to different stages at life, probably.

[]: Re: Shin as a conclusion to EoE             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Mar 23, 2021 4:39 am Axx°N N. wrote: I don't think EoE is about how "everything sucks,"

[]: Re: Shin as a conclusion to EoE             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Tue Mar 23, 2021 5:06 am The difference is that the affirmation isn't shown to be an ongoing process. The affirmational moments are treated like total resolutions and solutions. EoTV and EoE come to a conclusion that's more positive and healthy, but they don't do so by advising you erase the negative; they treat it as a necessary component. But Shin seems preoccupied with treating defining characteristics solely as problems that need to be removed. Shinji doesn't seem like he's coming to an understanding that will allow him to have meaningful relationships with other characters, but that he's finding the exact right words to both change them into completely different people and then never have to interact with them again in the first place. If the point was "learning to affirm others," I don't get the meaning behind Shinji leaving everyone behind. I also don't find that it makes sense to age him into a less introverted person. There's no impression that the process is ongoing. How will he affirm Mari exactly? What does she need to be affirmed about? The last image we have is a confident Shinji running away from anything that carries even a hint of negative connotations. Are we supposed to take the lingering shot of Kaworu as an implication he might someday get whistful? Then why no Asuka shot? It works for me only if it's Anno's autobiographical sendoffs, a creator licking stamps and mailing off his characters forever, but it's just not good character writing. Their veracity as human beings gets swallowed up into this pointless need to understand them in a final & ultimate sense. It doesn't strike me as mature, it feels like turning a blind eye in a narrative sense, dumping things unjustifiably, and presenting things as more conclusive than they are in reality.

[]: Re: Shin as a conclusion to EoE             Quote Post by Pluto » Tue Mar 23, 2021 5:26 am I don't think so but if you watch shin you will definitely get some additional context and winks on the first viewing. The first time I saw it, I was thinking how it sat nicely on top of the series but now I see it as its own thing. Both are good though. Sometimes I think Q was a bit of a sequel to EoE but that's just speculation (and probably for another topic)

Would you accept it as the conclusion? How would you feel if it was? Would you feel let down ? Indifferent?
